coredata.dc:title: Engineering quantum carbon dots unveiling quantum wave entanglement wave function on enamel substrate: A relativistic in-vitro study
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc. on behalf of The Academy of Dental Materials.

coredata.dc:creator: Daood, Umer
Amalraj, Fabian Davamani
Kaur, Kanwardeep
Bapat, Ranjeet Ajit
Seow, Liang Lin
coredata.dc:description:
Objectives As artificial atoms, quantum dots are widely used in quantum information research since their individual energy levels may be precisely controlled using gate voltages. The purpose of the study was to modify carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and evaluate its effects on the structure, crystal orientation and mechanical properties of the enamel substrate along with antibacterial properties of CQDs.
Materials and methods Enamel specimens of 4 mm × 4 mm × 3 mm were cut and CQD solution was dialyzed in deionized water mixed with urea solution and placed in microwave system (800 W) to obtain *CQD 0.1 % -, **CQD 0.2 % -, ***CQD 0.3 % -, and *****CQD 0.5 % for enamel blocks to be immersed for 2 weeks. X-ray diffraction analysis and density-functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed to determine degree of phase purity. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used for imaging of CQDs and treated enamel, with zeta potential measured with Zetasizer. Raman spectra was acquired with spectral range of 400–2000 cm-1. Atomic force microscopy was performed with a peak force set at 200 nN. Lactobacillus biofilm was prepared on treated enamel substrates and analysed using confocal, scanning electron microscopy and TEM.
Results DFT calculations summarised improved lattice parameters of HAp***CQD 0.3 % - and HAp***CQD 0.5 % -. Zeta potential is least for salineS and is maximum for *****CQD 0.5 % - distributed system. The salineS, and *CQD 0.1 % - groups had comparable v₁PO₄³⁻ value, indicating consistent phosphate intensities. TEM successfully verified carbon dots as spherical. Enamel crystals aligned their c-axis perpendicular to the electron beam within 1° with CQDs treated specimens exhibiting misoriented-crystals. *****CQD 0.5 % - group had highest elastic modulus and nano hardness with maximum shear stress. Calculated bond length and angles using XRD show higher measures (p < 0.05) in all CQD groups. *****CQD 0.5 % - exhibited a fibre texture pattern with an orientational distribution resembling an angle distortion. Most bacteria in the biofilms fluoresced red in CQD groups with no colony chain formations observed with *****CQD 0.5 % - group. CQDs assemblies were observed to cause explosive lysis through loss of cell integrity.
Conclusion *****CQD 0.5 % - modified enamel substrate displayed significant crystallite changes providing a novel option for fabrication of diverse functional CQDs aimed at modification of enamel tissue while possessing optimum antimicrobial properties.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematics showing the surface charge patterns of CQDs with insets of characterisation diagrams. On the left is the unit cell of hydroxyapatite from the calculated atomic positions with the local environment of Ca in pure HAp using VESTA 3 and Schrödinger small-molecule drug discovery suite. Bond distances are in Å.

(A) The energy levels of each quantum dot were regulated to achieve vibrational modes in QDs. The background-corrected patterns were subsequently converted from polar to Cartesian coordinates (azimuthal angle χ vs d-spacing) utilising these calibration values integrating over all azimuthal angles with the data in this format produces the one-dimensional diffraction pattern. (B) Representative Raman Spectra of experimental specimens (salineS, *CQD
0.1 %
- , ** CQD
0.2 %
- , *** CQD
0.3 %
- , and ***** CQD
0.5 %
- ) showing phosphate vibrational mode (960 cm⁻¹). Diffraction pattern in panel (hand arrow) plotted as corresponding diffraction pattern generated by integrating over indexed peaks for the hydroxyapatite structure.

TEM successfully verified the dimensions and shape of the (Fig. 3A–B, L) CQDs. The TEM pictures indicated that the carbon dots were predominantly (Fig. 3A–B) spherical. The HRTEM revealed an (Fig. 3B) interplanar spacing of 0.265 nm, consistent with the usual value documented in the literature. (C–D) SalineS control specimens in contrast to bright field TEM observations of enamel crystals seen in CQD modified specimens. Fig. F presents the optimal TEM image of *CQD 0.1 % - acquired for enamel crystallites. Fig. G illustrates the comparison of TEM pictures of ** CQD 0.2 % - with their respective control group counterparts. (H–I) depicted enamel crystals resulting major conformational change after ** CQD 0.3 % - modification with irregular spacing. The TEM images of (J–K) ***** CQD 0.5 % - exhibited noticeable alterations. Crystallite boundaries appear as either (J) strip stretch or can be identified by the (K) varying orientations of the crystallite lattices within the disordered interrod regions (dotted circle).

(A–B) The stress distribution in enamel induced by indentation at a depth of 0.28 μm: (B) inset of nano indent imprints on specimens. (C) Summary of data obtained for mechanical property measurements of experimental specimens. Nano hardness, elastic modulus, and material volume (p < 0.05) expressed as mean ± standard deviation. Values with different letters and numerals are significantly different (p < 0.05).

A high-resolution TEM picture of all experimental groups is presented demonstrating the primary inorganic HAp constituent. (B) ***** CQD 0.5 % - exhibit a fibre texture pattern with an orientational distribution in presence of the CQD. In the overview images (C-D), many of the crystals resemble an angle distortion arranged in a linear formation. In Figure D, only few crystals were chosen to expose the small angle region. (E-F) Vertical, pronounced bright-dark stripes represent the crystal lattices of hydroxyapatite encompassing both crystal reflections and small angle region. The associated (5 G) TEM images demonstrate densely packed crystals, with CQD can be observed. (H-I) The TEM image indicates as-prepared CQDs have an equal size distribution.

The usual morphologies of Lactobacilli are shown in Fig. 7 A–B. The bacteria treated with salineS ( Fig. 7 A–B) remained intact, dense and smooth, while those treated with *CQD

0.5 %
- modification, no colony chain formations were observed amongst the specimens ( Fig. 7 F), alongside the highest detachment noted. The concentration and type of modification employed influenced aggregation (p < 0.05).

TEM imaging of bacteria (A–C) demonstrated that the bacterial cells maintained their morphology, intracellular components (C; green arrow), and a cohesive cell wall (A; green arrows). TEM microscopy (D) indicated that CQDs were quasi spherical particles aggregation entailed chain blocks. The particles assembled, in sometimes increasing lengths interacting with bacterial cell wall as seen in (E) *CQD

The inset graphical image in G–H is an indication of the influence of the negative field environment controlled by the presence of the ***** CQD particles. The consequence of the wave field generated is reflected in the reduced growth of the biofilms. This is justified by the bacterial annihilation seen especially in ***** CQD
0.5 %

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As artificial atoms, quantum dots are widely used in quantum information research since their individual energy levels may be precisely controlled using gate voltages. The purpose of the study was to modify carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and evaluate its effects on the structure, crystal orientation and mechanical properties of the enamel substrate along with antibacterial properties of CQDs.

Enamel specimens of 4 mm × 4 mm × 3 mm were cut and CQD solution was dialyzed in deionized water mixed with urea solution and placed in microwave system (800 W) to obtain *CQD 0.1 % -, **CQD 0.2 % -, ***CQD 0.3 % -, and *****CQD 0.5 % for enamel blocks to be immersed for 2 weeks. X-ray diffraction analysis and density-functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed to determine degree of phase purity. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used for imaging of CQDs and treated enamel, with zeta potential measured with Zetasizer. Raman spectra was acquired with spectral range of 400–2000 cm-1. Atomic force microscopy was performed with a peak force set at 200 nN. Lactobacillus biofilm was prepared on treated enamel substrates and analysed using confocal, scanning electron microscopy and TEM.

DFT calculations summarised improved lattice parameters of HAp***CQD 0.3 % - and HAp***CQD 0.5 % -. Zeta potential is least for salineS and is maximum for *****CQD 0.5 % - distributed system. The salineS, and *CQD 0.1 % - groups had comparable v₁PO₄³⁻ value, indicating consistent phosphate intensities. TEM successfully verified carbon dots as spherical. Enamel crystals aligned their c-axis perpendicular to the electron beam within 1° with CQDs treated specimens exhibiting misoriented-crystals. *****CQD 0.5 % - group had highest elastic modulus and nano hardness with maximum shear stress. Calculated bond length and angles using XRD show higher measures (p < 0.05) in all CQD groups. *****CQD 0.5 % - exhibited a fibre texture pattern with an orientational distribution resembling an angle distortion. Most bacteria in the biofilms fluoresced red in CQD groups with no colony chain formations observed with *****CQD 0.5 % - group. CQDs assemblies were observed to cause explosive lysis through loss of cell integrity.

*****CQD 0.5 % - modified enamel substrate displayed significant crystallite changes providing a novel option for fabrication of diverse functional CQDs aimed at modification of enamel tissue while possessing optimum antimicrobial properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data, code, and resources utilized in the study are accessible to any researcher for the aim of replicating or expanding upon the analysis. The datasets produced and/or examined during the present investigation are not accessible to the public owing to confidentiality concerns. However, interested parties may get them from the corresponding author upon a reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: The dental enamel is the most mineralised tissue in the human body. The remarkable mechanical property of this structure allows it to endure the conditions found in the mouth, including masticatory cycles and biting forces of up to 770 N [1], as well as the incredibly high and low temperatures and pH levels experienced by the human body. A hard mineral called carbonated hydroxyapatite (HAp) makes up most of the structure, with small amount of water and a soft organic matrix making up the remaining 1 % and 4 %, respectively, found within the mature enamel [2,3]. The mechanical properties of enamel result due to its complex organisation as a hierarchical nanocomposite material [4,5]. The structure is aligned with carbonated apatite crystals that are believed to be orientated with their c-axes along the rod axis with mis-oriented and co-oriented orientation of crystalline c-axes. Moreover, the aligned orientation describes the morphological alignment of elongated crystals. The typical width of rod crystals in adult enamel is approximately 50 nm, although prior reports indicate that they are more than 10 μm long [6]. Inter rods, which are connected to each rod, are made up of crystals stacked at an angle of approximately sixty degrees to the rod axis [7]. The direction of crystal elongation changes progressively as one moves from the rod to the inter rod with organic matrix encasing each rod [8–12].

Changes in demineralisation and remineralisation occur rapidly throughout the progression of dental caries [13]. Bacteria in the plaque, like S. mutans, cause dental cavities, producing a very acidic environment with a pH below 5.5. This is due to the fermentation of organic acids leading to demineralisation of the tooth's hard tissue [14,15]. Clearly, specific carious lesions on teeth will develop if net demineralisation happens and the balance tips towards that. So, remineralisation of enamel is a good approach to minimally invasive dentistry for both preventing and treating early stages of caries [16,17]. Unless there is a modification-breakthrough, available remineralisation agents are not appropriate for clinical application due to the lengthy time it takes for their mediated enamel remineralisation processes [18,19]. Given the unevenness and diverse heterogeneity of analysed studies, it is difficult to establish the superiority one remineralizing agent over another [20]. Enamel hardness correlates with the extent of crystal misorientation, as demonstrated that small misorientations deflect cracks better than larger or no misorientations [21]. Since crack deflection is a common toughening mechanism [22], we can infer that the observed misorientations are mechanically significant in enamel, as they enhance the nanoscale enamel hardness, which is critically required for resisting the intense masticatory forces [23].

Quantum information processing relies on non-local correlations, and quantum entanglement is seen as a key resource for this process [24,25]. Atomic structures [26], and semiconductor quantum dots [27], have shown generation and control of entanglement. As artificial atoms, quantum dots (QDs) are widely used in quantum information research since their individual energy levels may be precisely controlled using gate voltages [28]. The formation of electron entanglements was investigated by means of a single-level quantum dot coupled to a single input and two output leads [29]. A driving field from outside the system is used to study the entanglement creation process in quantum dots. To show that this system can be controlled by an applied field, the dynamical generation of entangled states is investigated using an external potential difference [30]. A variety of targeted materials are extensively utilised, including bio ceramics comprising hydroxyapatite or calcium phosphate [31]. Nonetheless, these materials are proficient in facilitating mineralised regeneration, yet they do not permit real-time dynamic monitoring in vivo or in vitro. Real-time dynamic monitoring is vital for optimising treatment strategies, mitigating potential complications, and enhancing treatment efficacy. Unlike traditional ceramics and polymers, the hydroxyl groups on the surface of quantum dots can directly interact to facilitate the remineralization and regeneration [32]. Quantum dots can also demonstrate exceptional antibacterial characteristics, rendering them effective materials for the prevention or treatment of prevalent infections [33]. Carbon based dots strongly attract calcium ions, leading to the rapid production of calcite clusters [34]. To express this relativistic quantum interference for predictable characteristics of entanglement, spatially separated substrates or systems is still a rare possibility.

The goal of this paper is to make the issue of entangled spacelike separated systems in the oral environment, and to show how solving the quantum measurement problem in terms of the states that physical substrates possess and to track those changes is essential to understand entangled systems dynamics. Herein, we explain the issue of using quantum modified functionalized carbon quantum dots with strong negative charge displaying different surface charge pattern with entangled spacelike separated systems as precisely as possible and to show how changes can be brought on enamel surface using entangled systems. In actual experiment, the energy states of the carbon quantum dots are quantized using two metallic electrodes controlling each energy levels (data not shown). Therefore, the aim of the study was to fabricate new carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and evaluate its effects on structure and crystal orientation hypothesizing that enamel hardness correlates with the extent of crystal misorientation, motivating the analysis. In addition, the study determined the systemic regulation of the antibacterial activities of carbon quantum dots by modifying their surface charge. The null hypothesis was both crystal and surface structure of enamel had no significant changes in addition to presence of any antibacterial properties.

The project obtained permission from the Institutional Review Board of IMU. Obtained written informed consent from all persons who contributed extracted teeth for the laboratory in-vitro experiments were approved by the Institutional Research Ethical Committee (4.12/JCM-285/2024). According to the guidelines of the United States center for disease control (CDC), the extracted teeth were sterilized by autoclaving and storage in 10 % formalin before using for research purposes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of enamel blocks and demineralization
ce:section.ce:para: Forty extracted single rooted teeth were used for the study. Three samples of mm × 4 mm × 3 mm enamel specimens were used in the pilot study, which had a statistical power of 80 %, to determine the sample size. Radiographs were taken of each tooth. Teeth used in the experiments were devoid of cavities, and hypoplastic enamel. Using a periodontal curette, the teeth were quickly cleansed to remove any connective tissue following extraction. An Edenta AG diamond disc was used to cut and the roots. The enamel blocks, measuring 4 mm × 4 mm × 3 mm, were sectioned and maintained at room temperature for thirty days in a solution of 0.5 % thymol. The blocks were cut into 1 mm sections from edge to edge after the enamel has been polished. For artificial carious lesion preparation, specimens were preserved in a solution of pH of 5.3, added with 6 mM methylhydroxydiphosphonate to 50 mM acetic acid, 3 mM calcium chloride, 3 mM potassium bicarbonate, and 2 mL of water. The solution was replaced once a week, and the pH was monitored periodically. Silicon carbide grinding papers (Buehler Met; Agar Scientific Limited, Cambridge, UK) with progressively finer grits (300–2000) were used to polish the samples' surfaces under running water with minimal hand pressure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs (carbon quantum dots) and wave solution
ce:section.ce:para: A 10-milliliter beaker containing a solution of ammonium citrate (0.1 g) was heated in an oven at 180 °C for 2 h. Once it cooled to ambient temperature, the resulting black residue was cooled and later dissolved in a beaker of sodium hydroxide solution (100 mM, 10 mL). Before being centrifuged at 35,000 g relative centrifugal force (RCF) for 1 h, this solution was sonicated (1 h). The CQD solution (5 mL) ultrapure water was dialysed in deionised water (about 1 L) using a dialysis membrane (MWCO½1.0 kDa; Float-A-Lyzer G2, Spectrum Laboratories, Rancho Dominguez, CA) and stored at 4 °C. The water was replaced with ultrapure water every 60 min. 3 g of urea with 10.5 g of citric acid were filled inside a 50 mL volumetric flask containing 2 g of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP; TCI Japan). It was mixed with 18.2 MU cm of Milli-Q ultrapure water and mixed thoroughly. The previous dialysed solution was mixed with the urea solution and placed in a microwave system (CEM, Mars 6; MIMOS laboratory, KL, Malaysia) and set at 800 W and heated to 200 °C for 15 min. After the microwave reaction, the Teflon tubes were left in the machine for 30 min to cool down. The solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm pore size filter. Subsequently, the CQDs solution underwent dialysis using a membrane with a molecular weight cutoff of 3500 Daltons for 4 h. The solution was then stored at 4 oC till its use. 0.5 % concentrated CQD solution was prepared using 0.5 g of residue in 100 mL of the solution. Subsequently, additional solutions were prepared through straightforward dilution (0.2 % corresponds to a 2 × dilution, 0.1 % equates to a 5 × dilution, and so on). The control specimens included the isotonic 0.9 % saline solution (vehicle control) stored in sterile container. The charged technique entails the utilisation of carbon-rich sources, such as the presence of citric acid and urea as electrodes. The generation of CDs in electrochemical oxidation is typically accomplished by the anodic oxidation process. The quantum yield was obtained by applying 5 V to the working solution of different concentrations for 3 h. The enamel block specimens (n = 10) were divided into 4 groups (control salineS, *CQD 0.1 % -, **CQD 0.2 % -, ***CQD 0.3 % -, and *****CQD 0.5 % -) in each of the treatment solutions, soaking for about 3 weeks. The specimens were dried in vacuum chamber and presented for characterisation procedures. Precautions were taken to prevent any contamination.

An X-ray diffraction analysis was performed to determine the degree of phase purity in the as-enamel specimens (n = 10). The crystals were also visualised using Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP) and Schrödinger small-molecule drug discovery suite simulation software to simulate the compounds and conduct density-functional theory (DFT) calculations. We used a plane wave energy cut off of 750 eV. Highly compact Monkhorst-Pack k-point were used to sample the Brillouin zone of HAp with dimensions of 2 × 2 × 2. It was permitted for all atoms to relax to their equilibrium state locations until all atomic forces are less than 0.01 eV Å−1. All assumptions were made HAp as a hexagonal crystal structure (space group P6 3/m ) and 44 atoms per unit cell and lattice parameters a = b ≠ c and α = β = 90
o
, Υ = 120
o
. This would depict the unit cell and the local environment of Ca in pure HAp [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and zeta potential of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy (JEOL 3010 TEM, JEOL USA, Peabody, MA) was used to acquire Bright-field (BF) TEM images of the CQDs, and an average diameter was calculated from 100 counts. The TEM machine was operated at 200 kV. The carbon dots were drop casted on the carbon coated 300-mesh copper grid and vacuum dried before imaging. Selected area electron diffraction patterns were acquired using a diffraction aperture with a diameter of 500 nm. The CQDs' zeta potentials (ζ) were measured with a Zetasizer (Nano ZS, Malvern Instruments, Worcestershire, UK) using the identical refractive index and absorption coefficient of 2.14 and an equilibration duration of 120 s. The measurement period in each instance depended upon the number of runs, which ranged from 20 to 40. The measurements were conducted in triplicate using automatic attenuation and voltage selection settings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Raman mapping of enamel substrate after CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Raman spectra from each specimen (n = 10) were acquired by means of a near-infrared laser (785 nm) on a Horiba XploRA Confocal Microscope. The spectra were obtained using a 1200 lines/mm grating with a single window centred at 900 cm1 since evaluating the intensity of the band at approximately 960 cm−1 was the primary objective. With a spectral resolution of 4 cm−1, the examined spectral range was between 400 cm−1 and 2000 cm−1. At the outset of every measurement campaign, a Si-wafer standard was used to quantify energy calibration and band intensity. For every window, the accumulation number was 10 and the exposure time was 5 s. Ten measurements were taken on average for each sample. To eliminate the fluorescent background, spectra deconvolution was carried out using LabSpec (v5.58.25, Horiba, France), which makes use of a polynomial baseline correction. Area beneath the bands was integrated to get the intensities. To establish the Degree of Demineralisation (DD), the intensity of the PO4 3-band was measured using a neutral density filter with a 50 % efficiency produced a spatial resolution of 1 μm with an incident power on the sample of 5.0 ± 0.4 mW. A 100 × objective with a numerical aperture of 0.9 was also utilised.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transmission electron microscopy of enamel substrate after CQDs modification
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy (JEOL 3010 TEM, JEOL USA, Peabody, MA) was used to acquire TEM images of the treated enamel specimens (n = 10). Ultra-thin sections were cut (Ultracut UCT, Leica, Vienna, Austria) and specimens stained with 5 % uranyl acetate for 10 min and saturated lead citrate for 15 min. Once the enamel sections were examined, the TEM grids of the specimens were further exposed to 0.1 N HCl for 5 s. Following this, specimens were thoroughly rinsed with distilled water to eliminate any dissolved mineral components. High-angle annular dark field pictures were acquired to examine the Z-contrast of the samples.

The experiment was conducted using a Multimode 8 Atomic Force Microscope (Bruker, Billerica, Massachusetts, USA). The probe used was an RTESPA-525, manufactured by Bruker probes and having a nominal spring constant of around 200 N/m with a resonance frequency of approximately 525 kHz. To determine the deflection sensitivity and the PF-QNM synchronisation distance, the AFM setup was calibrated using the relative approach utilising sapphire. The Sadar approach was used to evaluate the real spring constant with each probe. A sample of control enamel specimens (n = 10) were used to calibrate the tip radius. A speed of 2 kHz was specified, the fast scan axis angle was 90°, and the maximum peak force was set at 200 nN. A maximum scan frequency of 0.150 Hz and a picture resolution of 256 × 256 pixels were chosen. Nanoscope Analysis software (Bruker, Palaiseau, France) automatically corrected the recorded force-distance curves, and the indentation modulus is derived from the Derjaguin-Muller-Toporov (DMT) contact model modified on the unloading part of the curve. Consequently, the "raw" value of the indentation modulus was preserved. Topographic images were scanned obtained at approximately x1 Hz on surfaces from 5 × 5 to 1 × 1 µm.

The powder X-ray diffraction patterns were obtained (n = 10) using a Bruker D2 phaser X-ray diffractometer, which operates at 30 kV and 10 mA and employs Cu Ka radiation. Diffraction patterns were acquired from 10 to 120° over 2θ, with a scan rate of 4 s per step and a step size of 0.01°. The Rietveld method, in conjunction with GSAS and the EXPGUI interface, was used to refine the powder patterns.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enamel crystal distortion evaluated by mapping
ce:section.ce:para: The X-ray microdiffraction analysis was carried out at the Advanced Photon Source (APS) beamline 34 ID-E. The 2D diffraction patterns were acquired in transmission on a MAR165 area detector using monochromatic X-rays (17 keV) focussed to a probe size of 500 nm 500 nm. The specimen (n = 10) was set up at the beam's focus point on a translational stage that could move x, y, and z with a precision of 250 nm. An efficient exposure length of 60 s per pattern was achieved by correlating diffraction patterns acquired with two 30-s integrations to eliminate erroneous gamma ray counts. Over the enamel region, a 2D map was created using 500 nm isotropic steps and 943 (23 × 41) patterns. This map includes patterns via air, FIB platinum, the copper grid, and the sample mounting process. They also gathered a calibration pattern with a powdered cement (CeO2) benchmark (NIST).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lactobacillus biofilm preparation: TEM and SEM evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: Lactobacillus MRS broth was procured from Difco Laboratories, Detroit, MI, USA, and Lactobacillus was utilised for biofilm production with a 1.5 McFarland turbidity level as baseline bacterial suspension. Bacteria were anaerobically grown for a 20-h period on LB-MRS agar plates at 37 °C. Incubation was carried out at 37 °C for 48 h and further added with BHI broth that had been treated with 8 % sucrose (pH 7.4) and varying doses of xylitol (0–2 %). Centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 15 min collected the bacterial cells after incubation, and 3 washes with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, 0.01 M, pH 7.2) were performed on the cell pellets. 100 mL of the growth media was used to resuspend the cells, and their concentration was adjusted to 10 × 9 cells/mL according to McFarland standard number. The bacterial cells were fixed for 90 min at 25 °C in a solution that contained 2 % w/v glutaraldehyde and 2 % w/v paraformaldehyde in 100 mM sodium cacodylate buffer (SCB) with a pH of 7.4. Afterward, the samples were rinsed twice with phosphate buffered solution and post-fixed in 2 % w/v osmium tetroxide for 1 hr at 4 °C. Dehydration was carried out using 30, 50, 70, 85, 95, 100 % v/v ethanol, followed by two washes, and finally dry ethanol at 4 °C. The Durcupan A/M epoxy resin (Sigma-Aldrich) was used to polymerise the samples for 48 hrs at 70 °C. After obtaining thin sections of 70–100 nm using a LKB 2088 Ultrotome V and staining them with 2 % uranyl acetate, observation was carried out using a TEM (JEOL 3010 TEM, JEOL USA, Peabody, MA) at 80 kV (n = 10).

Data collected was stored, cleaned, and analyzed using SPSS version 29.0 to screen for errors, outliers, or missing data. The mean and standard deviation of the test and questionnaire was calculated. The decision criteria set for rejection of the null hypothesis is at a p < 0.05 probability level.
The schematic representation of experiments is presented in Fig. 1.

For DFT calculations, Rietveld refinements were applied to the specimen’s atomic locations. Table 1 summarises the improved lattice parameters of HAp*** CQD

0.3 %

Different location sites displaying calculated geometry parameters of different groups are displayed in Table 1. The statistical test performed after a significant result from ANOVA (P-O (1)) identified ***CQD 0.3 % - and *****CQD 0.5 % - groups within the data as significantly different from each other by comparing all possible pairs of group means. There were significant differences for CQD groups in other bond lengths between **CQD 0.2 % -, ***CQD 0.3 % - and *****CQD 0.5 % - groups as depicted in Table 1.

Table 2 enumerates the values of mean zeta potential, and electrophoretic mobility, for pure and CQDs-dispersed specimens. Table 2 illustrates the fluctuation in the zeta-potential distribution profile according to the concentration of CQDs dispersion. The absolute value of zeta potential is at its least for salineS and at its maximum for the ***** CQD 0.5 % - distributed system. The zeta potential for all scattered systems is evidently higher than that of control specimens. The concentration dependencies of electrophoretic mobility are also presented in Table 2. The electrophoretic mobility is again maximised at a ***** CQD 0.5 % - dispersion of CQDs. The net surface charge on CQD is negative, as indicated by the negative zeta values.

Table 3 displays the intensity of v₁PO₄³⁻ (960 cm⁻¹) and v₁CO₃²⁻ (1071 cm⁻¹), as well as the v₁CO₃²⁻/v₁PO₄³⁻ ratio, which characterises the quality and composition of crystals with and without modification. A one-way ANOVA was conducted to evaluate the differences in Raman V1PO4 3- (960 cm−1) and V1CO3 2-/ V1PO4 3- ratio across the groups (p < 0.05). The salineS, and *CQD 0.1 % - groups had differences in v₁PO₄³⁻ values (314 ± 9.2 and 399 ± 7.7, respectively), indicating generally consistent phosphate intensities. Nevertheless, the **CQD 0.2 % - group exhibited a markedly reduced v1PO4 3- intensity (433 ± 5.5), potentially suggesting a reorganisation or alteration of the phosphate structure. The F-statistical value for **CQD 0.2 % - group was 31.3. The ***** CQD 0.5 % - group demonstrated the highest ratio (489 ± 4.1), greatly exceeding the other groups (p < 0.05). The background-corrected patterns were subsequently converted from polar to Cartesian coordinates (azimuthal angle χ vs d-spacing) utilising these calibration values integrating over all azimuthal angles with the data in this format produces the one-dimensional powder diffraction pattern. Fig. 2 B illustrates the representative micro-Raman spectra obtained from the enamel surfaces across all experimental groups. The symmetric v1 P-O band stretch was recorded at 960 cm⁻¹, whereas the asymmetric stretch, denoted as ν3 P-O, was represented at 1045 cm⁻¹. The symmetric stretch of the carbonate ion is similarly attributed to 1071 cm⁻¹ and may come from an overlap. The spectral response of hydroxyapatite is largely consistent across all groups. ce:section.ce:section-title: Transmission electron microscopy of enamel substrate
ce:section.ce:para:
TEM successfully verified the dimensions and shape of the (
Fig. 3 A–B, L) CQDs. The TEM pictures indicated that the carbon dots were predominantly ( Fig. 3 A) spherical. The HRTEM revealed an ( Fig. 3 B) interplanar spacing of 0.265 nm, consistent with the usual value documented in the literature. Although HAP crystals typically exhibit growth along the c-axis, it has been presumed that enamel crystals adhere to this growth orientation; the TEM images provide compelling evidence of this distribution within the ( Fig. 3 C–D) salineS control specimens in contrast to bright field TEM observations of enamel crystals seen in CQD modified specimens. The specimen long axis and c-axis, which is justifiable, as enamel crystals have predominantly been examined through electron diffraction in TEM with crystals aligning their c-axis perpendicular to the electron beam within 1°. All crystals in the salineS control specimens remain unchanged from their original state. The various orientations identified in each image ( Fig. 3 C–E) align completely with the normal crystal orientation. TEM on thin sections of enamel specimens treated with CQDs exhibit changes and misorientation within crystals. Fig. 3 F presents the optimal TEM image of *CQD
0.1 %
- acquired for enamel crystallites, with indications of a change in orientation as indicated by the arrow. Fig. 3 G illustrates the comparison of TEM pictures of ** CQD
0.2 %
- with their respective control group counterparts. The dark region in the image depicted corresponds to the change in the crystal structure at modification used with ** CQD
0.2 %

0.3 %

Consequently, it is evident that the enamel crystallite also exhibited an increase in its lattice parameter relative to the HAP unit cell. The TEM images of ( Fig. 3 J–K) ***** CQD
0.5 %

Fig. 4 shows the elastic modulus (GPa), hardness (GPa), and changes in material volume (μm3), for all testing groups. The *** CQD
0.5 %
- group had the highest elastic modulus (87.8 ± 5.1 GPa), and nano hardness measured (7.5 ± 1.2). Furthermore, ** CQD
0.2 %
- , *** CQD
0.3 %
- groups display similar maximum hardness and modulus values (p < 0.05). When the change in material volume (μm3) is considered ( Fig. 4 C), the maximum shear stress increases by 3.5 at an indentation depth for *** CQD
0.5 %
- group. The stress distribution pattern is similarly altered by plastic change, and the volume occupied by higher stress is dramatically reduced ( Fig. 4 A–B). As a result, increasing the stress level has increased material volumes caused by a possible shear sliding between mineral crystals due to conformational changes ( Fig. 4 A). Post-hoc pairwise comparisons using Tukey’s HSD, and Bonferroni revealed that **CQD 0.2 % -, ***CQD 0.3 % -, and *****CQD 0.5 % -, groups had significant differences in nano-hardness and Young’s moduli when compared to control and *CQD 0.1 % - groups. The effect size (η2) was calculated to assess the magnitude of the observed differences which showed significant differences for *****CQD 0.5 % -, groups.

The theoretical lattice constants from previous DFT calculations are a = b = 9.33 Å and c = 6.94 Å (mean). Table 4 show comparisons between atomic locations and relaxed bond lengths in the unit cell (diffraction analysis) with experimental and untreated enamel. Ca ions have nearest neighbouring oxygen atoms and three second-nearest adjacent oxygen atoms, but phosphate has just one second-nearest adjacent oxygen atom. The calculated bond length and angles show higher significant measures (p < 0.05) in all CQD groups as compared to the control group.

TEM imaging of bacteria (
Fig. 8 A–C) demonstrated that the bacterial cells maintained their morphology, intracellular components (dotted circles), and a cohesive cell wall (green arrows). TEM microscopy ( Fig. 8 D) indicated that CQDs were quasi spherical particles aggregation entailed chain blocks. The inset graphical image in Fig. 8 G–H is an indication of the influence of the negative field environment controlled by the presence of the ***** CQD particles. The consequence of the wave field generated is reflected in the reduced growth of the biofilms. This is justified by the bacterial annihilation seen especially in ***** CQD

The notion of quantum entanglement was derived from the Einstein–Podolsky–Rosen (EPR) dilemma, as elucidated by Einstein and colleagues. Quantum field generated changes has persistently been a crucial focus in the foundational elements. Unlike the traditional method, quantum information processing, associated with the famous entanglement, offers an expanded range of information manipulation skills [36,37]. The dominant theory, in our experiment, however, asserts that quantum entanglement or wave affect is merely a particular form of quantum correlation [38,39]. The relationship between classical and quantum elements may be more fundamental and comprehensive than the cosmic theory of entanglement. This study substantially integrates the quantum electrodynamics, offering a unique physical framework for analysing the interplay between fields generated and changes within the substrate. This concept connects two- or multi-level structures, and correlations examining the dynamics of quantum correlation in an oral environment. Specifically, we examine circular CQDs subjected to a linearly and circularly negatively polarised pulse and symmetries, respectively. Graphene or carbon-based dots exhibit minimal spin-orbit coupling and weak hyperfine interaction, which efficiently mitigate spin decoherence, making it an exceptional option for spin qubits. In recent years, significant efforts have been directed towards the realisation of authentic CQDs with bound states due to their potential application in spin [40–42]. Prior research indicates that carbon dots, encompassing CQDs and graphene quantum dots can be functionalised with various chemicals or functional groups for purposes such as labelling, sensing, tissue imaging, and antibacterial applications [43]. Therefore, identifying the functional groups of the CQDs is crucial for subsequent applications.

HAp, consists of hydroxylated calcium phosphate (Ca10(PO4)6(OH)2), is a widely researched calcium phosphate polymorph. Furthermore, HAp demonstrates ferro-, pyro-, and piezoelectric characteristics, which have practical applications in bone regeneration and growth, as well as in immuno sensing [44,45]. The hexagonal HAp structure adheres to two crystal facets: the a, b-plane and the c-plane [46]. The c-plane (or P site) primarily exposes PO4 3- and OH- ions, while a greater concentration of surface Ca2+ occupies the a, b-plane (or C site). As a result, a net negative charge remains on the c-plane, while a net positive charge is present on the a, b-plane, attributable to the system's anionic and cationic richness, respectively [47]. This allows the HAp system to display anisotropic features due to its variably charged facets, rendering HAp an optimal contender for the specified applications and surface changes. The potential for the exchange of Ca2+ and PO4 3- lattice ions render hydroxyapatite a flexible material for the selective extraction of anionic and cationic substances from the surrounding environment [48]. The appropriate use of this facet pertains to the regulated development or change of crystals orientation. The efficacy of the hexagonal crystal structure is enhanced by regulating growth along the c-plane or a, b-plane, as evidenced by the overall Ca/P ratio and microstructural changes. The augmentation of the exposed surface area of a target plane enhances the crystal's capacity for adsorption and ionic species exchange. Usually, the excitation-dependent negative charges of CQDs reflects the presence of QDs possessing different hybridized compositions and structures, and a distribution of different surface states [49].

DFT calculations reveal that the Ca site is the most possible site for changes. Site occupancies cannot be accurately evaluated experimentally due to the absence of diffraction data gathered with synchrotron X-rays or neutron diffraction. Nonetheless, XRD data have demonstrated that DFT reliably predicts the most energetically favourable site occupancy, wherein the Ca site is typically producing conformational changes [50,51]. The atomic locations compared and optimised bond lengths in the unit cell with experimental data and DFT results from previous studies closely align with the experimental data [52]. DFT studies demonstrate that the Ca site is the most energetically advantageous location for all evaluated replacements. The Density functional theory (DFT) reliably predicts the most energetically favourable site occupancy, wherein the Ca site is typically substituted or replaced. Table 1 presents the computed lattice parameters of which align closely with experimental data, especially for XRD results. The discrepancies in bond lengths and bond angles, especially within the ***** CQD
0.5 %
- specimens, leading to minor structural distortions/misorientations, as illustrated in TEM images ( Fig. 3 & 5 ). The negative zeta potential of the CQDs indicates that the electrostatic forces between the molecules are repulsive. A larger negative charge results in a higher repulsive force between the molecules. Consequently, the dispersed systems will exhibit greater stability, such as the ***** CQD
0.5 %
- scattered system. Table 2 presents the concentration dependencies of electrophoretic mobility for CQDs. The electrophoretic mobility is maximised at ***** CQD
0.5 %
- dispersion. Therefore, the zeta potential, and electrophoretic mobility, of CQDs-dispersed solution systems can be ascribed to various electrostatic and dispersion interactions, which may have had a profound effect on the enamel substrate. The net surface charge on CQD is negative. Consequently, CQDs in the medium, suffer electrostatic repulsive interactions between CQD molecules, indicating the presence of additional electrokinetic interactions [53], especially within the ***** CQD
0.5 %
- specimens. At a concentration of ***** CQD
0.5 %
- of CQDs, clusters of CQDs form, resulting in dominating dipolar interactions resulting in higher mobility, and may be the potential cause for the discrepancies found on the enamel surface. Corroborating further, the TEM images taken of enamel crystals indicated planar defects or angle distortions and did not split the crystal in any way. Consequently, it was logical to deduce that there was a change in the enamel structure with interplanar distances and lattice parameters changes, as indicated by the XRD investigation. The configuration of crystals in such a manner, inside the enamel structure, influences its mechanical properties [54]. When the hydroxyapatite crystals in enamel are non-uniformly orientated, as demonstrated by TEM, a consistent distribution of plastic behaviour results in post-yield strain hardening. This attribute contributes to damage tolerance and fracture mitigation [55]. We propose that the configuration of crystals post-modification may improve energy dissipation while maintaining adequate stiffness and mechanical properties ( Fig. 4 ). The minor misalignments facilitate fracture deflection between adjacent nanocrystals, whereas the organic materials between the rods offer a crack deflection mechanism at the rod level59. At elevated structural tiers, decussation patterns, the arrangement of various enamel types, and the effect of functionalised CQDs may itself contribute to improved mechanical performance.

Raman and XRD analyses are potent techniques for verifying apatite crystallinity and structure. The XRD data showed elevated crystallinity levels, which also suggest the presence of PO4 3- ions due to conformational changes as mentioned above. The phosphorus atoms exhibit vacancies that can be substituted, preserving charge balance following the loss of PO4 3- due to demineralisation [56]. In Raman analysis, the vibrational frequency of the v1 PO4 band is higher in ***** CQD
0.5 %
- and **** CQD
0.3 %
- specimens. The presence of CQD s can influence the displacement of the ν1 PO4 band near 960 cm−1, whereas the phosphate bands remain in a stable area. In this work, the phosphate mode (1071 cm⁻¹) manifested within the same wavenumber range without notable shifts. The carbonate ( ν1 CO3 2-) to phosphate ( ν1 PO4 3-) ratio is the predominant metric employed to assess enamel mineral quality. The carbonate/phosphate ratio (1070 cm⁻¹/960 cm⁻¹) seen in our data was dramatically diminished in the ***** CQD
0.5 %
- and **** CQD
0.3 %
- specimens ( Table 3 ), indicating an elevated presence of type B carbonate substituent in the enamel hydroxyapatite. This positively correlates with the diminished likelihood of enamel structure fracture. The carbonate/phosphate ratio seen through Raman measurement can serve as a dependable predictor of enamel mineral quality. Given that enamel substrates treated with CQDs possesses a distinctive Raman spectrum, the structural changes were evident in TEM images, together with the unequivocal results from Raman spectra, confirms the effect of the materials. Furthermore, direct substitution of Ca and CO3 2- may result in biological hydroxyapatite which contribute to variations in numerous structural, physical, chemical, and biological properties of HAp, including its morphology, lattice parameters, crystallinity, thermal stability, and solubility as also mentioned in previous studies [57,58]. Consequently, the null hypothesis was rejected due to significant alterations in the mechanical and structural changes of enamel following the application of CQDs in comparison to control groups.

Fracture deflection occurs at a reduced misorientation of 14.1°. Consequently, the minor misorientation angles within crystals observed in all TEM images and conformational changes within XRD evaluations may enhance crack deflection and, as a result, yield greater fracture toughness compared to larger misorientation angles. The link between hardness and misorientation observed indicates that greater misorientation corresponds to increased enamel hardness [59]. This link between hardness and elastic modulus with misorientation is depicted in Fig. 4 demonstrating a structure-function relationship that has not been previously discussed. Discontinuous mechanical characteristics at interfaces are known to deflect cracks [60]. The discovered misorientations or HAp distortions, although modest, are a prevalent nanoscale characteristic among vertebrate species and presumably enhance the mechanical performance of enamel. The fracture deflection processes are similarly hierarchical in organisation. The minor misalignments provide fracture deflection between neighbouring nanocrystals, whilst the organic materials interspersed throughout the rods enable a crack deflection mechanism at the rod level. At elevated structural tiers, decussation patterns, the arrangement of various enamel types, and the morphology of the tooth itself contribute to improved mechanical performance [61,62].

The CQDs in our study are governed by negative charged transitions which may predominantly be amorphous or more graphitic, including those commonly designated as “graphene quantum dots” in the literature [63,64]. Fig. 7 & 8 present characterisation results for the established CQDs wherein the diminutive carbon particles are surface passivated through functionalisation. Existing research indicates that the primary mechanisms underlying the antibacterial actions of CQDs are likely linked to the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS). Fig. 8 illustrates the mechanism of action, which encompasses the adhesion of CQDs to the bacterial surface, the disruption and infiltration of the bacterial cell wall/membrane, the induction of possible oxidative stress resulting in damage to DNA/RNA, leading to modifications or suppression of critical gene expressions, and the initiation of oxidative damage to proteins and other intracellular biomolecules [65,66]. In addition, the positively charged S-layer component associated with the cell envelope of lactobacillus species and the area implicated in S-layer assembly have been characterised in the following S-layer proteins: in the SA protein of Lactobacillus acidophilus ATCC 4356 [67]. The inhibitory effect was ascribed to the multivalent interactions of the negatively charged dots with the positively charged bacterial S layered membrane by binding to various nucleic acids, proteins, porins, and/or peptidoglycans. The integration of CQDs into a restorative material effectively diminished bacterial proliferation. Considering that the predominant reason for the necessity of restoration replacement is caries surrounding the restoration, it is logical to assert that the prevention of biofilm formation, particularly at the tooth/restoration interface, may aid in the prevention of caries adjacent to restorations. Nevertheless, the conditions examined herein, and the accessible data preclude any definitive conclusions in this regard. Another issue is the duration of the antibacterial effect observed. The long-term evaluation of antimicrobial activity is therapeutically significant, and the standardisation of antimicrobial testing for restorative materials is essential for accurate comparison of their antimicrobial qualities.

While modified carbon dots have been shown to positively influence on enamel substrate, the precise mechanism by which CQDs promote these changes within a restorative material remains unexplored. Furthermore, we have not established a connection between their effects and the surface motifs of the CQDs within a restorative material such as dental composite, nor have we systematically clarified this relationship. In-vitro investigations are required to examine the effects of CQDs within these light cure-based materials like dental composites. There should be a robust protocol and evaluation on depth of cure, degree of conversion, microleakage, bonding strength to enamel and dentin, and compatibility with various adhesive systems. Researchers are urged to perform strong in situ experiments to examine the effects of human saliva and oral surroundings on the antibacterial, mechanical, and physical properties of materials, especially using ****CQD 0.5 % - under relevant and changing oral environmental conditions.

Considering the limitations of this in vitro study, it can be inferred that ***** CQD
0.5 %
- modified enamel substrate and displayed significant crystallite changes. To the best of our knowledge, our manufacture of CQDs is the inaugural instance of a new variation of functional carbon dots exhibiting functionality and changes within enamel substrate due to fields generated. The carbon quantum dots presented in this paper proposes a novel methodology for the fabrication of diverse functional CQD probes aimed at modification of enamel tissue and possessing antimicrobial properties. As previously mentioned, the formulation has also affected the crystal size, especially at ***** CQD
0.5 %
- modified concentration, which demonstrated changes within the crystallite size and became conducive for enamel and dentin bonding with desired biocompatibility.
